title: Macular Degeneration (AMD)
oneLiner: Chronic, progressive degeneration of central retina (macula) causing irreversible vision loss; common in >50s; wet type is treatable emergency.

sections.Age-Related Macular Degeneration (AMD): **One-liner:** Chronic, progressive degeneration of central retina (macula) causing irreversible vision loss; common in >50s; wet type is treatable emergency.
sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Giant Cell Arteritis (GCA):** Sudden, painless vision loss + headache + jaw claudication + elevated ESR/CRP → immediate high-dose steroids (1g IV methylprednisolone) to prevent bilateral blindness and stroke.
- **Central Retinal Artery Occlusion (CRAO):** Rapid painless vision loss + afferent pupillary defect + cherry-red spot on fundoscopy → need emergency ophthalmology ± thrombolysis window (within 4-6 hours).
- **Acute Angle-Closure Glaucoma:** Painful red eye + fixed mid-dilated pupil + cloudy cornea + rock-hard globe → emergency topical pressure-lowering (acetazolamide) and urgent ophthalmology referral.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** Painless central vision loss (gradual in dry, sudden in wet), metamorphopsia (straight lines appear wavy), central scotoma (dark/blank spot), difficulty reading or recognising faces.
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Dry AMD: drusen (yellow deposits under retina), pigmentary changes (macular), geographic atrophy (sharply demarcated retinal thinning).
  - Wet AMD: subretinal fluid (blister), intraretinal fluid (cystoid spaces), subretinal haemorrhage, or a grey-green membrane (choroidal neovascularisation – CNV).
  - Amsler grid distortion (patient reports wavy/missing lines).
  - Reduced visual acuity (Snellen) with no improvement on pinhole.
  - Normal intraocular pressure, no conjunctival injection.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Giant Cell Arteritis:** ESR/CRP markedly elevated, temporal artery tenderness, headache, jaw claudication.
- **Central Retinal Vein Occlusion (CRVO):** Diffuse retinal haemorrhages ("blood and thunder"), not limited to macula; less frequent metamorphopsia.
- **Diabetic Macular Oedema (DMO):** History of diabetes, microaneurysms, hard exudates; OCT shows intraretinal fluid but no CNV.
- **Central Serous Chorioretinopathy (CSCR):** Younger patients (30-50), male predominant, often stress/high cortisol; OCT shows serous retinal detachment without CNV; no drusen.
- **Epiretinal Membrane / Macular Hole:** Metamorphopsia + fine, crinkled retina on OCT; no drusen; less acute onset.
- **Splitters:** OCT is the critical test – presence of subretinal fluid + CNV = wet AMD; no CNV + drusen + geographic atrophy = dry AMD. Elevated ESR/CRP (>50 mm/h) + new vision loss → GCA until proven otherwise.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** Snellen visual acuity (each eye separately), pinhole test (to rule out refractive error), Amsler grid (patient self-test for metamorphopsia), red desaturation test (if GCA suspected), check blood pressure.
- **Bloods:** FBC, ESR, CRP (mandatory in all new vision loss in >50 to screen for GCA). If GCA suspected: temporal artery ultrasound or biopsy (do not delay steroids).
- **Imaging:** First-line: OCT (optical coherence tomography) – gold standard; shows drusen, subretinal/intraretinal fluid, CNV, geographic atrophy. Second-line: fundus fluorescein angiography (FFA) or OCT angiography to visualise active CNV.
- **Special tests:** Colour vision (Ishihara) – often reduced; contrast sensitivity.
- **Diagnostic criteria:** No single scoring system for AMD; classification: Early AMD (drusen only), Intermediate AMD (large drusen >125µm ± pigment changes), Late AMD (wet: CNV; dry: geographic atrophy).
- **Never-miss test:** ESR and CRP in any patient over 50 with new, unexplained vision loss – GCA is a treatable emergency that can cause permanent bilateral blindness.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):** If suspected wet AMD → urgent ophthalmology referral (same-day to within 1 week depending on local protocol); no specific ED treatment. If GCA cannot be excluded → give IV methylprednisolone 1g (or oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg) immediately after bloods. If acute angle-closure glaucoma → 2% pilocarpine drops + acetazolamide 500mg IV/PO + urgent ophthalmology. If CRAO → ocular massage, timolol drops, discuss thrombolysis.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):** AMD patients rarely admitted unless complication (e.g., large subretinal haemorrhage). If admitted for anti-VEGF injection (ranibizumab, aflibercept, bevacizumab) – does not usually require overnight stay. No ward-level monitoring for AMD itself.
- **Long-term / outpatient:** Wet AMD: intravitreal anti-VEGF injections – frequency varies (monthly or treat-and-extend). Dry AMD: no proven treatment; lifestyle modification (smoking cessation, antioxidant vitamins AREDS2 formulation: lutein/zeaxanthin, vitamins C/E, zinc). Low vision aids (magnifiers, CCTV). Refer to low vision clinic.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** Not routinely needed. If GCA: high-dose steroids (IV to oral taper) + gastric protection (PPI) + bone protection (bisphosphonate if long-term). VTE prophylaxis as per local policy if mobility reduced. No diet restrictions.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Anti-VEGF agents (wet AMD):** Ranibizumab (Lucentis®) 0.5 mg intravitreal injection monthly initially (dose per local protocol; [check local policy] for exact schedule). Aflibercept (Eylea®) 2 mg intravitreal injection monthly for 3 months then 2-monthly. Bevacizumab (Avastin®) 1.25 mg intravitreal injection (off-label but NHS used). No renal/hepatic adjustment needed for intravitreal use.
- **Antioxidant supplements (dry AMD):** AREDS2 formula – lutein 10mg + zeaxanthin 2mg + vitamin C 500mg + vitamin E 400IU + zinc 80mg. Not for wet AMD.
- **For GCA:** Methylprednisolone IV 1g od for 3 days then tapering oral prednisolone; or oral prednisolone 40-60mg od. PPI (omeprazole 20mg od) and calcium/vitamin D or bisphosphonate if >3 months steroid use.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:** No absolute contraindications to anti-VEGF aside from active ocular infection. Patients on warfarin/direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs) can still receive injection – risk of haemorrhage is low but discuss with ophthalmology.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Not required for intravitreal agents. For steroids, no dose adjustment.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** With anti-VEGF injections – wrong eye (always check side before injection), expired or contaminated vial (must use from aseptic unit), incorrect dose (verify with pharmacy). For GCA steroids – delaying first dose while awaiting biopsy is a mistake; start immediately after bloods.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Subretinal haemorrhage (can be massive, causing breakthrough vitreous haemorrhage) – urgent ophthalmology.
  - Retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) tear – usually during anti-VEGF injection.
  - Endophthalmitis (rare, <0.1%) – painful red eye, hypopyon within 24-48h post-injection – emergency.
  - Steroid-related (if GCA): hyperglycaemia, infection, psychosis, GI bleed.
- **Chronic:**
  - Geographic atrophy (dry AMD progression) – irreversible vision loss.
  - Disciform scar (end-stage wet AMD) – central scarring, no treatment.
  - Macular hole or pseudohole.
  - Depression, falls, fractures due to vision loss.
sections.Prognosis:
- Dry AMD: slow progression over years; 10-15% progress to wet. Geographic atrophy leads to gradual central vision loss; no treatment to reverse.
- Wet AMD: if untreated, 90% develop severe vision loss (≤6/60) within 2 years. With anti-VEGF therapy, ~30-40% gain ≥3 lines of vision; most stabilise. Need lifelong injections (1-10 per year). Non-smokers have better outcomes.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- Always check Amsler grid in clinic for any patient with new visual distortion – this is the simplest screening tool.
- In a patient >50 with acute vision loss, obtain ESR/CRP before any eye tests – GCA can be missed if you don't look.
- Refer suspected wet AMD the same day – time to injection affects visual outcome; within 14 days is ideal.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- Do not assume chronic vision loss is "just AMD" – consider GCA, retinal vein occlusion, diabetic oedema, especially if unilateral and acute.
- Do not prescribe oral antioxidants (AREDS2) for patients who already have wet AMD or geographic atrophy in both eyes – they won't benefit and may get adverse effects.
- Do not ignore painless vision loss as "nothing acute" – central retinal artery occlusion has a 4-6 hour window for treatment.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Sudden worsening of vision (drop in Snellen, new wavy lines), curtain/block in any part of vision, eye pain or redness (suggests endophthalmitis or glaucoma), new headache or jaw pain (GCA).
- **Follow-up:** Wet AMD: patients typically have their next injection scheduled (e.g., 4-8 weeks). Routine review in 3-6 months for dry AMD. Low vision assessment if vision ≤6/12.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **When to call senior / ophthalmology reg:** New sudden vision loss in one or both eyes, especially with afferent pupillary defect (suggests optic neuropathy or CRAO). Suspected endophthalmitis (red painful eye + hypopyon) – call ophthalmology immediately, consider tap/inject antibiotics.
- **When to call medical reg / consider GCA:** ESR >50 mm/h or CRP >50 mg/L + headache/temporal tenderness – start steroids and discuss with rheumatology/ophthalmology. NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >2 in any patient with possible stroke/GCA – escalate.

rawMarkdown:
## Age-Related Macular Degeneration (AMD)
**One-liner:** Chronic, progressive degeneration of central retina (macula) causing irreversible vision loss; common in >50s; wet type is treatable emergency.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Giant Cell Arteritis (GCA):** Sudden, painless vision loss + headache + jaw claudication + elevated ESR/CRP → immediate high-dose steroids (1g IV methylprednisolone) to prevent bilateral blindness and stroke.
- **Central Retinal Artery Occlusion (CRAO):** Rapid painless vision loss + afferent pupillary defect + cherry-red spot on fundoscopy → need emergency ophthalmology ± thrombolysis window (within 4-6 hours).
- **Acute Angle-Closure Glaucoma:** Painful red eye + fixed mid-dilated pupil + cloudy cornea + rock-hard globe → emergency topical pressure-lowering (acetazolamide) and urgent ophthalmology referral.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** Painless central vision loss (gradual in dry, sudden in wet), metamorphopsia (straight lines appear wavy), central scotoma (dark/blank spot), difficulty reading or recognising faces.
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Dry AMD: drusen (yellow deposits under retina), pigmentary changes (macular), geographic atrophy (sharply demarcated retinal thinning).
  - Wet AMD: subretinal fluid (blister), intraretinal fluid (cystoid spaces), subretinal haemorrhage, or a grey-green membrane (choroidal neovascularisation – CNV).
  - Amsler grid distortion (patient reports wavy/missing lines).
  - Reduced visual acuity (Snellen) with no improvement on pinhole.
  - Normal intraocular pressure, no conjunctival injection.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Giant Cell Arteritis:** ESR/CRP markedly elevated, temporal artery tenderness, headache, jaw claudication.
- **Central Retinal Vein Occlusion (CRVO):** Diffuse retinal haemorrhages ("blood and thunder"), not limited to macula; less frequent metamorphopsia.
- **Diabetic Macular Oedema (DMO):** History of diabetes, microaneurysms, hard exudates; OCT shows intraretinal fluid but no CNV.
- **Central Serous Chorioretinopathy (CSCR):** Younger patients (30-50), male predominant, often stress/high cortisol; OCT shows serous retinal detachment without CNV; no drusen.
- **Epiretinal Membrane / Macular Hole:** Metamorphopsia + fine, crinkled retina on OCT; no drusen; less acute onset.
- **Splitters:** OCT is the critical test – presence of subretinal fluid + CNV = wet AMD; no CNV + drusen + geographic atrophy = dry AMD. Elevated ESR/CRP (>50 mm/h) + new vision loss → GCA until proven otherwise.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** Snellen visual acuity (each eye separately), pinhole test (to rule out refractive error), Amsler grid (patient self-test for metamorphopsia), red desaturation test (if GCA suspected), check blood pressure.
- **Bloods:** FBC, ESR, CRP (mandatory in all new vision loss in >50 to screen for GCA). If GCA suspected: temporal artery ultrasound or biopsy (do not delay steroids).
- **Imaging:** First-line: OCT (optical coherence tomography) – gold standard; shows drusen, subretinal/intraretinal fluid, CNV, geographic atrophy. Second-line: fundus fluorescein angiography (FFA) or OCT angiography to visualise active CNV.
- **Special tests:** Colour vision (Ishihara) – often reduced; contrast sensitivity.
- **Diagnostic criteria:** No single scoring system for AMD; classification: Early AMD (drusen only), Intermediate AMD (large drusen >125µm ± pigment changes), Late AMD (wet: CNV; dry: geographic atrophy).
- **Never-miss test:** ESR and CRP in any patient over 50 with new, unexplained vision loss – GCA is a treatable emergency that can cause permanent bilateral blindness.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):** If suspected wet AMD → urgent ophthalmology referral (same-day to within 1 week depending on local protocol); no specific ED treatment. If GCA cannot be excluded → give IV methylprednisolone 1g (or oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg) immediately after bloods. If acute angle-closure glaucoma → 2% pilocarpine drops + acetazolamide 500mg IV/PO + urgent ophthalmology. If CRAO → ocular massage, timolol drops, discuss thrombolysis.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):** AMD patients rarely admitted unless complication (e.g., large subretinal haemorrhage). If admitted for anti-VEGF injection (ranibizumab, aflibercept, bevacizumab) – does not usually require overnight stay. No ward-level monitoring for AMD itself.
- **Long-term / outpatient:** Wet AMD: intravitreal anti-VEGF injections – frequency varies (monthly or treat-and-extend). Dry AMD: no proven treatment; lifestyle modification (smoking cessation, antioxidant vitamins AREDS2 formulation: lutein/zeaxanthin, vitamins C/E, zinc). Low vision aids (magnifiers, CCTV). Refer to low vision clinic.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** Not routinely needed. If GCA: high-dose steroids (IV to oral taper) + gastric protection (PPI) + bone protection (bisphosphonate if long-term). VTE prophylaxis as per local policy if mobility reduced. No diet restrictions.

### Drugs & Doses
- **Anti-VEGF agents (wet AMD):** Ranibizumab (Lucentis®) 0.5 mg intravitreal injection monthly initially (dose per local protocol; [check local policy] for exact schedule). Aflibercept (Eylea®) 2 mg intravitreal injection monthly for 3 months then 2-monthly. Bevacizumab (Avastin®) 1.25 mg intravitreal injection (off-label but NHS used). No renal/hepatic adjustment needed for intravitreal use.
- **Antioxidant supplements (dry AMD):** AREDS2 formula – lutein 10mg + zeaxanthin 2mg + vitamin C 500mg + vitamin E 400IU + zinc 80mg. Not for wet AMD.
- **For GCA:** Methylprednisolone IV 1g od for 3 days then tapering oral prednisolone; or oral prednisolone 40-60mg od. PPI (omeprazole 20mg od) and calcium/vitamin D or bisphosphonate if >3 months steroid use.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:** No absolute contraindications to anti-VEGF aside from active ocular infection. Patients on warfarin/direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs) can still receive injection – risk of haemorrhage is low but discuss with ophthalmology.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Not required for intravitreal agents. For steroids, no dose adjustment.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** With anti-VEGF injections – wrong eye (always check side before injection), expired or contaminated vial (must use from aseptic unit), incorrect dose (verify with pharmacy). For GCA steroids – delaying first dose while awaiting biopsy is a mistake; start immediately after bloods.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Subretinal haemorrhage (can be massive, causing breakthrough vitreous haemorrhage) – urgent ophthalmology.
  - Retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) tear – usually during anti-VEGF injection.
  - Endophthalmitis (rare, <0.1%) – painful red eye, hypopyon within 24-48h post-injection – emergency.
  - Steroid-related (if GCA): hyperglycaemia, infection, psychosis, GI bleed.
- **Chronic:**
  - Geographic atrophy (dry AMD progression) – irreversible vision loss.
  - Disciform scar (end-stage wet AMD) – central scarring, no treatment.
  - Macular hole or pseudohole.
  - Depression, falls, fractures due to vision loss.

### Prognosis
- Dry AMD: slow progression over years; 10-15% progress to wet. Geographic atrophy leads to gradual central vision loss; no treatment to reverse.
- Wet AMD: if untreated, 90% develop severe vision loss (≤6/60) within 2 years. With anti-VEGF therapy, ~30-40% gain ≥3 lines of vision; most stabilise. Need lifelong injections (1-10 per year). Non-smokers have better outcomes.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- Always check Amsler grid in clinic for any patient with new visual distortion – this is the simplest screening tool.
- In a patient >50 with acute vision loss, obtain ESR/CRP before any eye tests – GCA can be missed if you don't look.
- Refer suspected wet AMD the same day – time to injection affects visual outcome; within 14 days is ideal.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- Do not assume chronic vision loss is "just AMD" – consider GCA, retinal vein occlusion, diabetic oedema, especially if unilateral and acute.
- Do not prescribe oral antioxidants (AREDS2) for patients who already have wet AMD or geographic atrophy in both eyes – they won't benefit and may get adverse effects.
- Do not ignore painless vision loss as "nothing acute" – central retinal artery occlusion has a 4-6 hour window for treatment.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Sudden worsening of vision (drop in Snellen, new wavy lines), curtain/block in any part of vision, eye pain or redness (suggests endophthalmitis or glaucoma), new headache or jaw pain (GCA).
- **Follow-up:** Wet AMD: patients typically have their next injection scheduled (e.g., 4-8 weeks). Routine review in 3-6 months for dry AMD. Low vision assessment if vision ≤6/12.

### Escalation Criteria
- **When to call senior / ophthalmology reg:** New sudden vision loss in one or both eyes, especially with afferent pupillary defect (suggests optic neuropathy or CRAO). Suspected endophthalmitis (red painful eye + hypopyon) – call ophthalmology immediately, consider tap/inject antibiotics.
- **When to call medical reg / consider GCA:** ESR >50 mm/h or CRP >50 mg/L + headache/temporal tenderness – start steroids and discuss with rheumatology/ophthalmology. NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >2 in any patient with possible stroke/GCA – escalate.
